title: Database Systems — Staff+ FAANG Interview Prep
description: Comprehensive deep-dive into database systems at Staff+ FAANG engineering level. Covers storage engine internals, ARIES crash recovery, MVCC implementations, concurrency in storage engines, distributed SQL (Spanner, CockroachDB, FoundationDB), replication, partitioning, distributed transactions, query optimization at scale, advanced indexing (including vector/HNSW), HTAP columnar engines, observability, cloud-native databases, security, DBaaS engineering, caching, schema migration, zero-downtime operations, performance tuning, specialized engines, AI/ML databases, cost modeling, and interview strategy — every topic a Staff+ infrastructure engineer must own.
details:
Staff+ FAANG Interview Strategy: Database questions at Staff+ level go far beyond indexing basics and isolation levels. You are expected to: (1) Design the storage backend for a new global service — choose between B-tree, LSM, columnar, and defend with read/write ratios, latency SLOs, and hardware constraints, (2) Trace a transaction through the full system — from application request through query planner, concurrency control, buffer pool, WAL, replication, and storage, (3) Diagnose production incidents — slow queries, replication lag, MVCC bloat, checkpoint stalls, lock contention, (4) Optimize across layers — how application access patterns, OS configuration (huge pages, io_uring, dirty page tuning), and storage engine internals interact, (5) Make build-vs-buy decisions — when to use PostgreSQL vs DynamoDB vs Spanner vs a custom engine, with cost and operational complexity tradeoffs.

Company-specific emphasis: Google focuses on Spanner (TrueTime, F1, globally-distributed SQL), Bigtable, and their custom storage infrastructure (Colossus, Borg, GCP databases). Amazon emphasizes DynamoDB (single-digit ms at any scale), Aurora (disaggregated storage, quorum writes), RDS multi-AZ, and cost-efficient storage (S3-based tiering, EBS gp3). Meta dives deep on MySQL at scale (RocksDB/MyRocks, online schema changes with gh-ost), TAO (graph cache), distributed caching (Memcached), and their OSS contributions for database tooling. Netflix focuses on multi-region databases (EVCache, Cassandra, RDS cross-region), chaos engineering for databases (failure injection), and schema change automation. Uber pioneered sharded MySQL (Schemaless, Docstore), distributed SQL (CockroachDB migration), and real-time data ingestion pipelines.

Key principle: Staff+ database answers demonstrate deep internals knowledge + operational scars. "We migrated from auto-increment IDs to UUID v7 to avoid B-tree rightmost page contention on our 500K writes/sec insert workload. This eliminated a recurring p99 latency spike and saved us from a multi-AZ primary migration every 3 months."

sections.title: Storage Engine Internals: B-trees, LSM-Trees & Buffer Pool
sections.description:
STORAGE ENGINE · INDEX STRUCTURES · CACHING

Staff+ expectation: You must understand the full storage engine stack — from on-disk page layout through index traversal to buffer pool caching. Every FAANG Staff+ engineer should be able to explain the read/write amplification tradeoffs between B-trees and LSM-trees and defend which to use for a given workload.

B-tree Internals — Beyond the Textbook: PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB, Oracle all use B+-trees (data only at leaves). Key structural details: (1) Page size — typically 8KB (PostgreSQL) or 16KB (InnoDB). Page is the unit of I/O — reading one row reads the entire page. (2) Fill factor — InnoDB defaults to 15/16 (about 94% full); PostgreSQL defaults to 90%. Lower fill factor = more space but fewer page splits on insert-heavy workloads. (3) Page splits — when a leaf page is full, it splits into two pages. Rightmost-page splits (monotonically increasing keys) are worst — every insert touches the same hot leaf. Mitigations: Use UUID v7 (time-ordered) instead of v4 (random), or use hash-based sharding to distribute writes.

~3-4
B-tree seeks for 1B rows (fanout ~341 with 8KB pages)
10-100x
LSM write amplification vs B-tree (but 2-5x better write throughput)
4-8KB
Typical page size — unit of I/O, caching, and locking

LSM-Trees — Write-Optimized Design: LevelDB, RocksDB, Bigtable, Cassandra, Scylla, Yugabyte use LSM-trees. Write path: (1) Write to WAL for durability, (2) Write to memtable (sorted in-memory, often a skip list), (3) When memtable is full (~64MB), flush as SSTable to disk. (4) Background compaction merges SSTables across levels. Compaction strategies: (1) Size-tiered: merge SSTables of similar size. High read amplification (many SSTables to check). (2) Leveled: each level is 10x larger. Low read amplification O(log N), but higher write amplification (each key rewritten ~10-30x).

Buffer Pool Management: (1) PostgreSQL shared_buffers — typically 25% of RAM, CLOCK-sweep eviction. (2) InnoDB buffer pool — 70-80% of RAM, multiple LRU lists with midpoint insertion (prevents large scans from flushing the cache). Staff+ insight: A full-table scan can flush the entire buffer pool. Mitigations: InnoDB's adaptive LRU midpoint insertion; PostgreSQL set effective_io_concurrency high; use read replicas for analytical queries.
sections.codeBlock:
# Buffer pool and page tuning at FAANG scale

# PostgreSQL buffer pool (set to 25% of RAM for dedicated DB)
alter system set shared_buffers = '32GB';

# InnoDB buffer pool (70-80% of RAM)
innodb_buffer_pool_size = 64G
innodb_buffer_pool_instances = 8
innodb_old_blocks_time = 1000

# LSM compaction tuning (RocksDB/MyRocks)
# rocksdb_level_compaction_dynamic_level_bytes = ON
# rocksdb_max_bytes_for_level_multiplier = 10

sections.title: ARIES Crash Recovery & Write-Ahead Logging
sections.description:
CRASH RECOVERY · ARIES · WAL · CHECKPOINTING

Staff+ expectation: ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics) is the most influential recovery algorithm — used in PostgreSQL, Oracle, DB2, SQL Server. You must understand the three recovery phases, LSN (Log Sequence Number), and how WAL guarantees durability without flushing data pages on every transaction.

ARIES Three Phases: (1) Analysis: Starting from last checkpoint, scan log forward to rebuild transaction table (in-flight txns) and dirty page table (pages needing REDO). Only reads log, not data pages. (2) REDO: Replay log from recovery LSN to end. REDO is idempotent — page LSN tracks last applied change. Uses physiological logging: page-oriented REDO, log-oriented UNDO. (3) UNDO: Roll back uncommitted transactions. Writes Compensation Log Records (CLRs) for idempotent UNDO — if crash during UNDO, restart from last CLR.

Checkpointing: Fuzzy checkpoint (ARIES): Write checkpoint record with transaction table + dirty page table snapshot. Pages flushed asynchronously. PostgreSQL spreads flush over checkpoint_completion_target (default 0.5 x checkpoint_interval) to avoid I/O spikes. InnoDB uses adaptive flushing — if log fills fast (approaching innodb_log_files_total x 0.75), it flushes more aggressively. LSN: Each log record has a unique LSN. Each data page stores page_lsn — the LSN of the last modification. During REDO, if page_lsn >= log LSN, the change is already applied. Full-page writes (PostgreSQL, default on) write entire page contents to WAL after first post-checkpoint modification — handles torn pages from partial writes during crash.
sections.codeBlock:
# WAL and checkpoint tuning at FAANG scale

# PostgreSQL WAL configuration
wal_level = replica
wal_buffers = '64MB'
wal_writer_delay = '200ms'

# Checkpoint tuning
checkpoint_timeout = '15min'
checkpoint_completion_target = 0.9
max_wal_size = '128GB'
min_wal_size = '16GB'

full_page_writes = on

# InnoDB redo log
innodb_log_file_size = 4G
innodb_log_files_in_group = 4
innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit = 1

sections.title: Transaction Processing & MVCC Deep Dive
sections.description:
TRANSACTIONS · MVCC · CONCURRENCY CONTROL

Staff+ expectation: MVCC is the foundation of modern database concurrency control. You must understand how PostgreSQL's heap-page MVCC differs from InnoDB's undo-log MVCC, how SSI prevents write skew, and how FoundationDB implements OCC for deterministic conflict detection.

PostgreSQL MVCC — Tuple-Level Versioning: Each tuple has t_xmin (creating XID) and t_xmax (expiring XID). INSERT: t_xmin = current XID. UPDATE: mark old tuple t_xmax = current XID, insert new tuple with t_xmin = current XID. DELETE: set t_xmax = current XID. Visibility: a transaction sees a tuple if t_xmin is committed and in its snapshot AND t_xmax is 0 or not committed. Dead tuples accumulate with each UPDATE — VACUUM reclaims space. Tune autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor to 0.01 for OLTP (default 0.2 is too high).

InnoDB MVCC — Undo Log-Based: Stores only latest version in clustered index. Old versions in undo log. UPDATE: acquire exclusive lock, write old values to undo log (before image), update record with new values. Read view: snapshot of active XIDs at query start. If row was modified by active txn, follow undo log to reconstruct visible version. Key difference from PostgreSQL: InnoDB's undo log is more compact for updates to same row — one undo entry vs PostgreSQL's dead tuple. But long-running transactions prevent undo log cleanup (purge), causing undo tablespace growth.

SSI — Serializable Snapshot Isolation: Snapshot Isolation (PostgreSQL Repeatable Read) allows write skew — two transactions read overlapping data and write non-overlapping data, producing a non-serializable result. PostgreSQL SSI adds predicate locking (SIREAD locks on query ranges). If two transactions have overlapping SIREAD locks and both write, one is aborted. At high contention, abort rates can reach 20-30% — unsuitable for high-throughput OLTP but ideal for correctness-critical (financial ledgers).
sections.codeBlock:
# MVCC tuning at FAANG scale

# PostgreSQL autovacuum tuning for OLTP
autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.01
autovacuum_vacuum_threshold = 1000
autovacuum_naptime = '30s'
autovacuum_max_workers = 5

# Monitor MVCC bloat
SELECT schemaname, tablename, n_dead_tup, n_live_tup,
       round(n_dead_tup * 100.0 / (n_live_tup + n_dead_tup + 1), 2) AS dead_pct
FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC LIMIT 10;

# InnoDB purge tuning
innodb_purge_threads = 4
innodb_purge_batch_size = 300
innodb_max_purge_lag = 1000000

SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS;  -- check History list length

sections.title: Concurrency in Storage Engines: Latching & Locking
sections.description:
CONCURRENCY · LATCHING · LOCK-FREE B-TREES

Staff+ expectation: Understand the difference between locks (transaction-level, deadlock-detectable) and latches (internal page protection, short-lived). Know how latch coupling enables concurrent B-tree access, how optimistic latching reduces contention, and how B-link trees enable near-linear scalability.

Locks vs Latches: Locks: held for transaction duration (seconds/minutes), deadlock detection via waits-for graph, modes S/X/IS/IX/SIX. Latches: held for microseconds (page access only), no deadlock detection (code must avoid conflicting order), modes read/write (spinlock/mutex).

Latch Coupling (Crabbing): Traverse B-tree: (1) Acquire read latch on root. (2) Acquire read latch on child. (3) Release parent. (4) Repeat to leaf. For writes: acquire exclusive latch at root; if page is safe (won't split/merge), release parent exclusive; otherwise hold all parent exclusives down the path.

B-link Trees — Lock-Free Variant: Adds right-link pointers from each page to its right sibling. When a page splits, the left page links to the new right page before parent update. Readers follow right-link if they find the page split (detected by comparing high key with search key). Used in academic implementations; most production DBs (PostgreSQL, InnoDB) still use latch coupling with careful deadlock avoidance. Optimistic latching increasingly adopted: acquire leaf latch, read, verify page still consistent (LSN unchanged), retry if not.
sections.codeBlock:
# Monitor lock/latch contention

# PostgreSQL lock monitoring
SELECT pg_blocking_pids(pid) AS blocked_by, query, wait_event, wait_event_type
FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE wait_event IS NOT NULL;

# InnoDB lock monitoring
SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS;  -- check LATEST DETECTED DEADLOCK

# PostgreSQL LWLock contention
SELECT lw.lwlock_type, count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity a,
lateral (SELECT CASE WHEN a.wait_event LIKE 'LWLock%' THEN a.wait_event ELSE 'other' END AS lwlock_type) lw
WHERE a.state = 'active' GROUP BY lwlock_type;

sections.title: Distributed SQL & NewSQL: Spanner, CockroachDB & FoundationDB
sections.description:
DISTRIBUTED SQL · NEWSQL · GLOBAL CONSENSUS

Staff+ expectation: NewSQL combines horizontal scalability with ACID. Understand how Spanner uses TrueTime for external consistency, how CockroachDB uses HLC for serializable txns without TrueTime, and how FoundationDB's deterministic model enables aggressive optimization. Compare systems and recommend based on deployment constraints.

Spanner — Google (2012): First globally-distributed DB with externally-consistent transactions using TrueTime. TrueTime returns an interval [earliest, latest] guaranteed to contain actual time (GPS + atomic clocks, interval 1-7ms). Commit: wait TT.after(s) before making commit visible (~7ms). Paxos-based replication per tablet. Key innovation: TrueTime + concurrency control provides external consistency without distributed 2PC cost.

CockroachDB (2015): Serializable isolation using Hybrid Logical Clocks (HLC). Parallel commits reduce commit latency from 2 RTTs to 1 RTT. Encounter a write intent during read -> follow intent pointer -> if committed, use intent's value. Max clock offset default 500ms - nodes exceeding it are shut down.

FoundationDB (2013): Optimistic Concurrency Control with deterministic simulation testing. Client batches read/write sets. Master detects conflicts, assigns global commit order, replicates ordered batch. Deterministic simulation tested 10,000+ core-years before public release. Tradeoff: no SQL natively (Record Layer adds it), higher latency for multi-key transactions.

sections.title: Replication Architectures: Sync, Async, Quorum & CRDTs
sections.description:
REPLICATION · CONSENSUS · CONSISTENCY

Staff+ expectation: Replication is the foundation of HA and fault-tolerant databases. Understand the full spectrum: async replication through quorum-based (Raft/Paxos) to CRDTs. Design a replication strategy meeting latency, durability, and consistency requirements of a global service.

PostgreSQL Streaming Replication: Primary ships WAL to standby. Modes: (1) Asynchronous — commit without standby ack. Best latency, data loss on primary failure. (2) Synchronous — wait for at least one standby WAL flush. Zero data loss, adds RTT latency. Settings: on (flush), remote_write (OS write, ~50us faster), remote_apply (standby has applied). Staff+ tip: Use remote_write for most cases — OS write survives datacenter power loss.

MySQL Group Replication: Multi-leader/single-leader using Paxos consensus. Group members agree on transaction order before commit. Conflicts detected at commit time (not via locks) — aborted for conflict. Adds 1-2 RTTs latency per transaction. Excellent for in-region HA but unsuitable cross-region.

Raft/Paxos for Replication: etcd, Consul, TiDB, Yugabyte use Raft. Raft properties: Strong leader for writes, log replication to majority, leader election on failure. Linearizable reads require leader to confirm it's still leader (quorum heartbeat). Solutions: follower reads (with staleness), Multi-Raft (TiDB/CockroachDB) — each range has its own Raft group/leader.

CRDTs — Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types: Each replica accepts writes independently, merges without conflicts. Types: G-Counter (grow-only), PN-Counter (vector clock), G-Set (union merge), OR-Set (observed-remove with tombstones). Foundation of Google Docs, Figma, Riak DB, Redis Active-Active.
sections.codeBlock:
# Replication monitoring at FAANG scale

# PostgreSQL replication lag
SELECT pid, application_name, state, sync_state,
       pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), replay_lsn) AS lag_bytes,
       now() - pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp() AS lag_time
FROM pg_stat_replication;

# MySQL replication lag
SHOW REPLICA STATUS;
# Use pt-heartbeat for accurate measurement

# etcd Raft status
etcdctl endpoint status --cluster -w table

sections.title: Partitioning, Sharding & Multi-Database Routing
sections.description:
SHARDING · PARTITIONING · DATA ROUTING

Staff+ expectation: Sharding is inevitable at FAANG scale. Understand partitioning strategies, rebalancing without downtime, cross-shard query handling, and how proxies (Vitess, ProxySQL) route queries. Also understand CQRS and event sourcing that reduce cross-shard coordination.

Sharding Key Selection: Best: customer/user ID (natural distribution, queries scoped to one customer), tenant ID for SaaS. Worst: timestamps (writes hotspot one shard), status booleans (terrible distribution). Schemes:

  • Range: users A-D → shard 1, E-H → shard 2. Simple, efficient range scans, but range skew creates hotspots. PostgreSQL declarative, MySQL RANGE, CockroachDB RANGE.
  • Hash: hash(key) mod N. Even distribution, range scans need broadcast. Adding shard remaps most keys — solved by consistent hashing (K/N keys move). DynamoDB partition key, Cassandra, Vitess hash vindex.
  • Directory: Lookup table maps key → shard. Flexible, custom routing. Downside: lookup table is single point of failure. Uber's Schemaless.

Re-Sharding at Scale: (1) Fixed shard count (Vitess, DynamoDB): N shards at start, split overflowing shards into two. (2) Consistent hashing with virtual nodes (Cassandra, Scylla): each node owns multiple tokens. Adding node assigns tokens from others — data moves in 0.5% increments. (3) Range-based rebalancing (Bigtable, CockroachDB): data split into tablets/ranges, load balancer moves hot ranges between nodes.

Cross-Shard Queries: (1) Broadcast join — send to all shards, aggregate. (2) Multi-shard 2PC — high latency, avoid. (3) Materialized views via MapReduce/Spark. (4) CQRS — separate write (append-only events) from read (materialized views). Event sourcing eliminates cross-shard consistency.
sections.codeBlock:
# Sharding operations at FAANG scale

# Vitess — reshard (split shard)
vtctlclient Reshard -source_shards=0 -target_shards=0-40,40-80,80-c0,c0-00 mykeyspace

# PostgreSQL partition management
CREATE TABLE users (id UUID NOT NULL, name TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW())
PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at);
CREATE TABLE users_2024_q1 PARTITION OF users
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-01-01') TO ('2024-04-01');

# Cassandra — check token ranges
nodetool status
nodetool ring

sections.title: Distributed Transactions & Consensus Protocols
sections.description:
DISTRIBUTED TRANSACTIONS · CONSENSUS · SAGAS

Staff+ expectation: Distributed transactions are the hardest problem in databases. Understand 2PC/3PC limitations, Percolator (Google's multi-row txns on Bigtable), Calvin (deterministic), Sagas for microservices, and how TrueTime simplifies the problem.

2PC: Coordinator asks all participants to prepare (fsync to WAL). If all ready, coordinator logs commit and sends commit. Blocking problem: If coordinator crashes after prepare but before commit, participants hold locks indefinitely. 3PC adds pre-commit phase to avoid blocking but requires non-failing network.

Percolator — Google (2010): Added ACID transactions to Bigtable. Uses global Timestamp Oracle (TSO). Two-phase commit with locks — client picks start timestamp, writes with locks (invisible to readers). Commit: picks commit timestamp, checks locks, writes acknowledge record, releases locks. Lazy conflict resolution: if client crashes, another txn can clean stale locks after timeout. Foundation of CockroachDB, TiDB, etc. Limitation: TSO becomes throughput bottleneck at extreme scale.

Calvin — Deterministic (FaunaDB): All transactions ordered upfront by sequencer. Participants execute deterministically — same sequence produces same state. No 2PC needed. Tradeoffs: must declare read/write sets upfront, sequencer is SPO, bounded by slowest participant.

Saga Pattern: Sequence of local transactions with compensating transactions. Choreography: services publish events, next service reacts. Orchestration: coordinator tells each participant what to do. No isolation — intermediate results visible. More practical than 2PC at FAANG scale (Netflix, Uber, Amazon use sagas for order processing, billing).

sections.title: Query Optimization at Scale: Cardinality, Cost Models & Learned Optimization
sections.description:
QUERY OPTIMIZATION · COST MODELS · ML FOR DB

Staff+ expectation: Query optimization bridges SQL semantics and execution performance. Understand cardinality estimation techniques, cost model internals, learned optimization (Neo, OtterTune), and automatic index recommendation.

Cardinality Estimation Methods: (1) Histograms — PostgreSQL stores equi-depth histograms (up to 10,000 buckets). Uniform distribution assumption fails for skewed data. (2) N-distinct statistics for GROUP BY. (3) Correlation statistics (ndistinct, dependencies) capture column correlations — without these, planner assumes independence and underestimates selectivity. (4) HyperLogLog for approximate distinct counts.

Learned Query Optimization: (1) Neo (Microsoft) — replaces histogram-based estimator with neural network trained on past executions. 30-40% reduction in execution time. (2) OtterTune (CMU/AWS) — ML for database knob tuning using workload classifier + Gaussian Process regression. (3) Automatic Index Recommendation — pg_qualstats + hypopg, Oracle SQL Tuning Advisor, Google Cloud SQL Index Advisor. Staff+ insight: Each index adds write overhead. At FAANG scale, an unused index on a 10TB table costs ~$100K/year. Automatic tools must consider write/read tradeoff.

Cost Model Internals: PostgreSQL uses: seq_page_cost=1.0, random_page_cost=4.0 (too high for SSDs!), cpu_tuple_cost=0.01, cpu_operator_cost=0.0025. Staff+ tip: Set random_page_cost=1.1 for NVMe SSDs — changes planner from sequential scans to index scans, improving point query performance 5-15x. PostgreSQL 17 added io_combine_limit for SSD parallel random I/O.

sections.title: Advanced Indexing: Partial, Covering, BRIN, Bloom, Vector & Learned Indexes
sections.description:
INDEXING · SEARCH · VECTOR · LEARNED

Staff+ expectation: Modern indexing goes far beyond B-trees. Know partial indexes for filtered queries, covering indexes for index-only scans, BRIN for append-only, bloom filters for multi-column equality, and emerging vector indexes (HNSW, IVF-PQ) and learned indexes.

Partial Indexes: Index on subset of rows — CREATE INDEX idx_active ON users(email) WHERE status = 'active'. Much smaller, lower write overhead. FAANG use: At Meta, every DB has partial indexes on hot partitions (e.g., WHERE deleted_at IS NULL) — cuts storage 70-90%.

Covering Indexes: Include all columns needed by a query — no heap access. PostgreSQL INCLUDE clause: CREATE INDEX idx_cov ON orders(user_id, created_at) INCLUDE (total_amount, status). 10-50x faster than heap lookups.

BRIN — Block Range Indexes: Stores min/max per block range (128 pages). 100x smaller than B-tree for time-series. False positives — good when data physically clustered by indexed column.

Bloomer Filter Indexes: PostgreSQL bloom extension for multi-column equality queries. Only supports equality (=), not range. False positives require table recheck.

Vector Indexes (pgvector): (1) IVF-Flat: cluster vectors into N centroids, search closest centroids. O(probes x vectors_per_cell). (2) HNSW: multi-layer graph. O(log N) search. Memory-intensive (stores full graph). Higher recall than IVF.

Learned Indexes (Kraska 2018): Replace B-tree pages with neural network predicting key position. 10-100x smaller than B-tree, 1.5-3x faster lookup. Limitations: Insertions require retraining (O(N)). Read-heavy, write-seldom workloads only (CDN index, catalog).

sections.title: HTAP & Columnar Engines: Row vs Column Stores, Vectorized Execution & Compression
sections.description:
HTAP · COLUMNAR · VECTORIZATION · COMPRESSION

Staff+ expectation: HTAP systems blur OLTP/OLAP lines. Understand columnar storage (Parquet, ORC), vectorized execution, compression (RLE, dictionary, delta, bit-pack), zone maps (min-max pruning), late materialization, and know when Snowflake/ClickHouse is better than a converged HTAP engine.

Row vs Columnar: Row stores: tuple-at-a-time iterator, efficient for single-row OLTP, page-level compression. Column stores: column batches (vectorized), only read needed columns (huge savings for wide tables), column-specific encoding (5-20x compression), inefficient for updates. Vectorized execution: Process batches of 1024-4096 rows at a time. Better CPU cache, SIMD (AVX2/AVX-512 processes 8-16 elements per instruction), reduced virtual call overhead. Adopted by ClickHouse, DuckDB, Snowflake, Spark.

Columnar Encoding: (1) RLE — for few repeated values. (2) Dictionary — map distinct to integers. (3) Delta — differences between values (sorted columns). (4) Bit-packing — minimum bits per value. Zone maps: Row groups (1M rows) store min/max per column. Query with date range can skip groups whose max < query_min — drastically reduces I/O. Late materialization: Apply filters and aggregations on encoded column values before reconstructing rows. Reduces CPU and memory.

sections.title: Observability, Monitoring & Capacity Planning
sections.description:
OBSERVABILITY · MONITORING · CAPACITY

Staff+ expectation: Database observability prevents outages. Design the monitoring stack: slow query analytics, replication lag tracking, WAL growth, MVCC bloat, connection saturation, storage forecasting, and distributed tracing correlation.

Slow Query Analysis: pg_stat_statements tracks cumulative stats per normalized query. Sort by total_time/calls for high-average-latency queries. auto_explain logs plans for queries above threshold. pgBadger produces HTML reports. MySQL slow_query_log + pt-query-digest identifies top-N queries. Staff+ insight: Regression detection is key — track mean/p99 execution time per query and alert on deviations >20%.

Replication Lag: PostgreSQL pg_stat_replication shows replay_lsn vs current WAL. MySQL Seconds_Behind_Master is famously unreliable (can report 0 when stalled). Use pt-heartbeat for accuracy. Storage Forecasting: Track daily growth GB/day, fit linear/exponential trend, project when storage hits 80% (warning) / 95% (critical). Staff+ insight: Separate bloat from genuine data. A table with autovacuum lagging can have 50% bloat — total storage overestimates growth.
sections.codeBlock:
# Database observability toolkit

# PostgreSQL — track WAL generation rate
SELECT pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), '0/0') / 3600) AS wal_bytes_per_hour;

# PostgreSQL — table size breakdown
SELECT relname, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid)) AS total,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_table_size(relid)) AS table_size,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_indexes_size(relid)) AS index_size
FROM pg_catalog.pg_statio_user_tables
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(relid) DESC LIMIT 20;

# Monitoring replication lag with pt-heartbeat
pt-heartbeat --update --daemonize --database=percona --interval=1.0
pt-heartbeat --check --database=percona --master-server-id=1

sections.title: Cloud-Native Databases: Aurora, AlloyDB, Neon, PlanetScale & Snowflake
sections.description:
CLOUD DATABASES · DISAGGREGATED STORAGE · SERVERLESS

Staff+ expectation: Cloud-native DBs disaggregate compute from storage. Understand Aurora (quorum writes, distributed storage), AlloyDB (columnar + PostgreSQL), Neon (serverless with branching), PlanetScale (Vitess-based), and Snowflake (multi-cluster shared-data). Compare architectures and recommend based on workload.

Aurora — AWS: 6 copies across 3 AZs. Write quorum 4/6, read quorum 3/6. Only WAL sent to storage — storage layer applies WAL to pages. No buffer cache warm-up on failover. Storage auto-scales 10GB to 128TB. Instant crash recovery (<60s). Backward (time-travel to any point in 72h). Tradeoffs: Higher latency than local NVMe (+200-500us for storage network), write amplification from 6-way replication.

Google AlloyDB: Regional distributed storage with integrated columnar engine. Writes go to row-oriented storage; columnar cache populated asynchronously for analytical queries. 4x faster analytical queries than standard PostgreSQL. Adaptive autovacuum using ML.

Neon: Fully decoupled compute + storage. Pageserver stores page snapshots + WAL. Compute is stateless, cold-starts in ~500ms. Supports git-like branching (instant database clone for dev/preview). Tradeoff: 500ms cold start unsuitable for latency-sensitive serving — excellent for dev/staging/CI.

sections.title: Database Security & Encryption
sections.description:
SECURITY · ENCRYPTION · COMPLIANCE

Staff+ expectation: Design defense-in-depth for database security: encryption at rest/in-transit, audit logging, row-level security, column-level encryption, key management, and compliance (GDPR, SOC2, PCI, HIPAA).

Encryption at Rest: (1) TDE — page-level encryption. Tablespace master key wraps page encryption key. Keys stored in AWS KMS / GCP Cloud KMS / HashiCorp Vault. Adds 5-15% CPU overhead. (2) File-level (LUKS, EBS encryption) — simpler, negligible overhead with AES-NI (10+ GB/s per core). Prefer file-level for performance, TDE for multi-tenant isolation with per-tablespace keys.

Encryption in Transit: TLS 1.3 mandatory. Certificate management: self-signed (simple, MITM-vulnerable) → CA-managed (cloud DBs) → mTLS (mutual, used for cross-region replication). TLS adds 1-5% CPU. Use AES-NI and session resumption to reduce handshake overhead.

Row-Level Security (RLS): PostgreSQL RLS: CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON users USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::INT). Eliminates risk of cross-tenant data exposure through application bugs. Overhead: 5-20%. For high throughput, prefer application-level WHERE filtering.

Column-Level Encryption: (1) Application-level — encrypt before writing to DB. DB sees encrypted blobs. Strongest (DBA cannot read data), but cannot index or operate on encrypted data. (2) pgcrypto — encrypt at SQL level. Key must be supplied to PostgreSQL — anyone reading server memory can read key.

Compliance: pgAudit for PostgreSQL, MySQL audit log. Key rotation: KMS-managed keys rotate automatically (annually). Manual rotation at petabyte scale is a significant project. GDPR right-to-erasure: soft-delete with deletion marker, async purge from backups.

sections.title: DBaaS Engineering & Resource Governance
sections.description:
DBaaS · MULTI-TENANCY · RESOURCE GOVERNANCE

Staff+ expectation: Design a DBaaS platform balancing tenant isolation with utilization. Automate provisioning/backup/upgrades. Implement governance: query budgeting, runaway cancellation, IOPS limits.

Multi-Tenancy Models: (1) Dedicated instance — strongest isolation, lowest utilization. (2) Shared instance, separate databases — better utilization, noisy tenant risk. (3) Shared database with RLS — highest utilization, no physical isolation. Hybrid approach at FAANG: Tenants needing guarantees get dedicated instances; others share with cgroup resource limits.

Resource Governance: (1) CPU — cgroup v2 cpu.weight for proportional, cpu.max for hard limits. (2) I/O — ionice -c 2 -n 0 for foreground queries, -n 7 for background tasks (vacuum, backup). systemd-run IOReadBandwidthMax for hard limits. (3) Memory — use memory.high (soft throttle) + DB-internal limits (work_mem, shared_buffers per tenant). (4) Connections — PostgreSQL max_connections, PgBouncer pool limits per tenant.

Zero-Downtime Upgrades: (1) Major version: pg_upgrade --link (hardlinks, seconds downtime for index rebuild). Blue-green: new instance streams WAL from old primary, DNS switch when caught up. (2) Minor: restart (30-60s). Mitigation: rolling restart with PgBouncer draining connections. (3) OS/Kernel: live migration (KVM), or kdump+kexec for fast reboot (~10s).
sections.codeBlock:
# DBaaS resource governance

# cgroup v2 CPU limits for a tenant
mkdir -p /sys/fs/cgroup/tenant1/
echo 100 > /sys/fs/cgroup/tenant1/cpu.weight
echo $PID > /sys/fs/cgroup/tenant1/cgroup.procs

# I/O priority for database processes
ionice -c 2 -n 0 -p $DB_MAIN_PID    # foreground queries
ionice -c 2 -n 7 -p $VACUUM_PID     # background vacuum

# PgBouncer per-tenant connection pooling
[databases]
tenant1 = host=127.0.0.1 port=5432 dbname=tenant1 pool_size=50

# MySQL resource groups
CREATE RESOURCE GROUP rg_oltp TYPE=USER VCPU=0-3 THREAD_PRIORITY=5;

sections.title: Caching Patterns & Materialization
sections.description:
CACHING · MATERIALIZATION · INVALIDATION

Staff+ expectation: Caching is the primary tool for reducing database read load at FAANG scale. Understand caching strategies, invalidation patterns, distributed cache consistency (Redis Cluster vs CRDT replication), and materialized views for precomputation.

Caching Strategies: (1) Cache-aside (lazy loading): app checks cache (hit → return), misses → query DB → populate cache. Most common, simple, cache has only requested data. (2) Read-through: cache library transparently queries DB on miss. (3) Write-through: write to cache first, then DB. Ensures cache consistency but adds write latency. (4) Write-behind (write-back): write to cache first, asynchronously write to DB. Low write latency but risk of data loss on cache failure.

Cache Invalidation: (1) TTL-based — simplest, eventual consistency. Works for most read-heavy workloads. (2) Write invalidation — delete cache entry on DB write. Requires cache-to-DB coordination. (3) CDC-based — listen to database change data capture (Debezium, Kafka Connect), invalidate/update cache entries for changed rows. Staff+ insight: At Meta's scale, they use CDC-based cache invalidation with Memcached — updates to MySQL generate CDC events that propagate to Memcached clusters within milliseconds.

Distributed Cache Consistency: (1) Redis Cluster — data partitioned across nodes via hash slots. No cross-slot transactions. Asynchronous replication (potential data loss on failover). (2) Redis Enterprise Active-Active — uses CRDT-based replication. Each replica accepts writes, merges automatically. (3) Materialized views — pre-compute and store query results in the database (PostgreSQL materialized views, ClickHouse materialized views). Refreshed on schedule or via triggers. Good for dashboards and reporting.
sections.codeBlock:
# Caching patterns at FAANG scale

# Redis cache-aside pattern (Python)
# def get_user(user_id):
#     user = redis.get(f"user:{user_id}")
#     if user:
#         return user
#     user = db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s", user_id)
#     redis.setex(f"user:{user_id}", 300, user)  # 5-min TTL
#     return user

# PostgreSQL materialized view
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW daily_sales AS
SELECT date_trunc('day', created_at) AS day,
       SUM(amount) AS total, COUNT(*) AS orders
FROM orders GROUP BY 1;

REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY daily_sales;

sections.title: Schema Design & Migration at Scale
sections.description:
SCHEMA DESIGN · MIGRATION · DATA BACKFILL

Staff+ expectation: Schema migrations at FAANG scale must be backward-compatible, reversible, and zero-downtime. Understand online DDL tools (gh-ost, pt-online-schema-change, Vitess VReplication), backward-compatible patterns (expand-contract), dual-write migration, and data backfill strategies.

Online Schema Change Tools: (1) gh-ost (GitHub): Creates a shadow table with the new schema, streams binlog changes from the original table, applies them to the shadow table incrementally, then atomically switches. No triggers, no locks. Can be paused/throttled. Handles 100K+ writes/sec during migration. (2) pt-online-schema-change (Percona): Uses triggers to capture changes. Simpler but trigger overhead impacts write performance. (3) Vitess VReplication: For sharded MySQL — VReplication streams changes from source shards to target shards during resharding/schema change.

Backward-Compatible Schema Patterns:

  • Add column: Add nullable column first (no default), then backfill data, then make NOT NULL with default. The application should not read/write the new column until released.
  • Rename column: Never do ALTER TABLE RENAME COLUMN. Instead: add new column, dual-write (write to both), backfill old data, migrate reads to new column, remove old column.
  • Change column type: Add new column with new type, dual-write, backfill, switch reads, drop old column (expand-contract pattern).
  • Remove column: Stop writing to it, let backend stop reading it over multiple releases, then DROP COLUMN.

Data Backfill: Backfilling millions of rows requires batching. Use UPDATE ... WHERE id BETWEEN :start AND :end LIMIT 1000 with exponential backoff on lock conflicts. Monitor replication lag during backfill — throttle if lag exceeds threshold. At FAANG scale, a 10TB backfill can take days.
sections.codeBlock:
# Schema migration at FAANG scale

# gh-ost — online schema change (no locks, no triggers)
gh-ost --alter="ADD COLUMN last_login TIMESTAMP NULL" \
       --database=myapp --table=users \
       --execute --switch-to-rename-table

# Vitess VReplication — resharding
vtctlclient VReplicationExec -keyspace mykeyspace -shard 0 \
  'INSERT INTO _vt.vreplication (workflow, source, pos) VALUES ...'

# Expand-contract pattern for column rename
# Phase 1: Add new column
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email_new VARCHAR(255);
# Phase 2: Dual-write (application writes to both)
# Phase 3: Backfill
UPDATE users SET email_new = email WHERE email_new IS NULL LIMIT 1000;
# Phase 4: Switch reads to email_new
# Phase 5: Drop old column
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email;

sections.title: Zero-Downtime Operations & Release Engineering
sections.description:
RELEASE ENGINEERING · ZERO-DOWNTIME · CI/CD

Staff+ expectation: Database releases must be zero-downtime, automated, and reversible. Understand connection draining, proxy-based failover, canary deployment, migration pipelines in CI/CD, SQL linting, and performance regression testing.

Connection Draining: Before restarting a primary for upgrade: (1) Set the database to read-only or reject connections. (2) PgBouncer pauses pool — stops accepting new connections, drains existing ones. (3) Wait for active queries to complete (statement_timeout prevents long-running queries from blocking). (4) Restart/upgrade primary. (5) Resume pool. Failover pattern: Use a proxy (HAProxy, ProxySQL, PgBouncer in transaction mode) with health checks. The proxy detects primary failure, redirects connections to the new primary (promoted replica). Client libraries should retry on connection errors.

Canary Deployments for Schema Changes: Roll out schema changes to a subset of instances (canary) before fleet-wide. Monitor: (1) Error rate (5xx responses), (2) p99 query latency, (3) Replication lag, (4) Lock contention. If any metric degrades >10%, rollback immediately. Automated rollback: Feature flags control read/write path behavior. If the new column causes issues, toggle the flag to stop writing to it.

CI/CD Pipeline for Databases: (1) SQL linting — SQLFluff, sqlcheck catch anti-patterns (SELECT *, missing WHERE on UPDATE, non-sargable predicates). (2) Schema drift detection — compare CI branch schema with prod schema using tools like schemadiff (Vitess) or pg_dump --schema-only diff. (3) Performance regression testing — run EXPLAIN on migrated vs original schema against production query logs (pgBadger replay). Flag plans with higher estimated cost. (4) Migration order — DDL must be applied before code that depends on it; DROP must be applied after code stops using it.
sections.codeBlock:
# Zero-downtime operations at FAANG scale

# PgBouncer — pause before restart
pgbouncer -R /tmp/pgbouncer.pdb PAUSE mydb
# Wait for active queries to finish
# Perform upgrade/restart
pgbouncer -R /tmp/pgbouncer.pdb RESUME mydb

# HAProxy health check for PostgreSQL primary
# backend pg-primary
#     option httpchk GET /health
#     server pg1 10.0.1.1:5432 check port=8008

# SQLFlint — CI linting
pip install sqlfluff
sqlfluff lint migrations/20240601_add_column.sql

sections.title: Performance Tuning & OS Interaction
sections.description:
PERFORMANCE TUNING · OS INTERACTION · KERNEL

Staff+ expectation: Database performance tuning requires understanding how the database interacts with the OS. Beyond knobs (shared_buffers, work_mem), you must understand I/O tuning (O_DIRECT, filesystem choice), NUMA-aware configuration, huge page usage, io_uring adoption, dirty page tuning, and OOM handling.

PostgreSQL Memory Tuning: shared_buffers (25% of RAM, default 128MB), effective_cache_size (50-75% of RAM — tells planner how much OS page cache is available), work_mem (per-query sort/hash memory — can be set per session; too high = OOM), maintenance_work_mem (for VACUUM, CREATE INDEX — set 2-4GB for fast index builds). Staff+ insight: work_mem times max_connections can exceed RAM. PostgreSQL does not pool work_mem — each connection allocates its own. Set work_mem modestly (4-16MB) and rely on temp files for large sorts on 99th percentile queries.

I/O Tuning: (1) Filesystem: XFS for PostgreSQL (recommended, concurrent direct I/O), ext4 for MySQL. Mount with noatime,nobarrier (if battery-backed RAID). (2) O_DIRECT: PostgreSQL's wal_buffers uses O_DIRECT (no OS cache for WAL — WAL fsyncs are deterministic). Data files use buffered I/O (OS page cache). InnoDB uses O_DIRECT for the tablespace (avoid double buffering — InnoDB has its own buffer pool). (3) io_uring: PostgreSQL 17+ supports io_uring for WAL writes and checkpoints. Reduces syscall overhead. (4) I/O scheduler: Use none (NVMe) or mq-deadline (SATA SSD).

NUMA-Aware Database Configuration: (1) Pin database process to NUMA-local CPUs and memory: numactl --cpunodebind=0 --membind=0. (2) Disable NUMA balancing (numa_balancing=0) — databases manage memory access patterns. (3) Configure buffer pool per NUMA node (InnoDB buffer pool instances = number of NUMA nodes). (4) PostgreSQL: disable NUMA in Postgres (numa_not_supported = on) — PostgreSQL's allocator (glibc malloc) is NUMA-aware by default.
sections.codeBlock:
# Database performance tuning at FAANG scale

# PostgreSQL memory tuning
shared_buffers = '32GB'           # 25% of RAM
effective_cache_size = '64GB'     # 50-75% of RAM
work_mem = '16MB'                 # per-query sort/hash
maintenance_work_mem = '4GB'      # for VACUUM/INDEX
wal_buffers = '64MB'

# InnoDB memory tuning
innodb_buffer_pool_size = 64G     # 70-80% of RAM
innodb_buffer_pool_instances = 8  # per NUMA node
innodb_log_buffer_size = 64M

# NUMA pinning
numactl --cpunodebind=0 --membind=0 postgres -D /var/lib/pgsql/data

# Filesystem mount for database data
# /dev/nvme0n1 /data xfs noatime,nobarrier 0 0

sections.title: Specialized Engines & Polyglot Persistence
sections.description:
SPECIALIZED ENGINES · POLYGLOT · TRADEOFF ANALYSIS

Staff+ expectation: Choose the right database for the workload — a Staff+ skill that avoids the "one database to rule them all" fallacy. Understand time-series, graph, vector, search, and ledger databases. Develop a tradeoff framework for selecting specialized vs general-purpose engines.

CategoryDB OptionsWhen to Use
Time-seriesInfluxDB, TimescaleDB, ClickHouseHigh write throughput with append-mostly patterns; range scans over time windows; downsampling/rollups
GraphNeo4j, DGraph, NeptuneDeep relationship traversal (social networks, recommendation, fraud detection, knowledge graphs)
VectorPinecone, Milvus, Weaviate, pgvectorSimilarity search for embeddings, RAG pipelines, semantic search; if <1M vectors, pgvector is sufficient
SearchElasticsearch, OpenSearchFull-text search, faceted search, log analytics, application monitoring (ELK stack)
LedgerAmazon QLDB, immutable tables in PostgreSQLAppend-only, cryptographically verifiable log (financial audit, supply chain, regulatory)

Polyglot Persistence Decision Framework: (1) Start with PostgreSQL — it handles 80% of use cases (JSONB for document, PostGIS for geospatial, pgvector for vector, full-text search). Only introduce a specialized engine when PostgreSQL hits a concrete bottleneck: write throughput (use TimescaleDB), relationship depth (use Neo4j), or vector scale >10M (use Pinecone/Milvus). (2) Operational cost: Each additional DB type adds operational complexity — expertise, monitoring, backup, failover procedures. The total cost of operating N databases is higher than the sum of parts due to cognitive load. (3) Migration cost: Switching databases requires data migration, query rewrites, and application changes. Avoid the "DB treadmill" — rewriting the same system for a new database every 2 years.
sections.codeBlock:
# Polyglot persistence evaluation

# When to use an OLTP database vs specialized:
# Use PostgreSQL/MySQL for:
# - Core business data (users, orders, payments)
# - ACID transactions required
# - Moderate read/write (<10K TPS per node)

# Use specialized engine when:
# - Time-series: writes >100K points/sec, need downsampling
# - Graph: >3 levels of JOIN depth with variable paths
# - Vector: >10M embeddings, need <10ms latency
# - Search: full-text + faceted + aggregations at <100ms

sections.title: Database for AI/ML Workloads
sections.description:
AI/ML · VECTORS · FEATURE STORES · EMBEDDINGS

Staff+ expectation: AI/ML workloads present unique database challenges: embedding storage and retrieval, feature serving at scale, training data management, and experiment tracking. Understand full-stack ML data infrastructure.

Vector Similarity Search at Scale: Embeddings (text, image, audio) are dense vectors (typically 256-4096 dimensions). Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search: Exact search is O(N*d) — impossible for 1B+ vectors. ANN indexes trade recall for speed: (1) HNSW: Multi-layer graph, O(log N) search. Best recall-speed tradeoff. Memory-heavy. (2) IVF-PQ: Inverted file + product quantization. Compresses vectors (reduces memory 4-16x). Lower recall than HNSW but much more memory-efficient. (3) Hybrid search (BM25 + vector): Combine keyword matching with semantic search. Services like Elasticsearch, Weaviate, and PostgreSQL with pgvector support hybrid scoring. Staff+ insight: For production RAG pipelines: store raw text in PostgreSQL, compute embeddings via an external service (OpenAI, Cohere), store in pgvector or Pinecone. The database is not the embedding computation engine — it's the storage and retrieval layer.

Feature Stores (Feast, Tecton): ML models require features computed from raw data. Feature stores centralize feature computation, storage, and serving. Online serving: Low-latency feature retrieval (<10ms) during inference — typically backed by Redis or DynamoDB. Offline serving: Batch feature computation for training — typically backed by BigQuery or S3 Parquet. Key challenge: Point-in-time correctness — training features must use the data that was available at prediction time (no future data leakage). Feature stores handle temporal joins to ensure this.

ML Data Versioning (DVC, LakeFS, Nessie): ML datasets need version control like code. DVC tracks data file hashes in Git, stores data in S3/GCS. LakeFS provides git-like branching for data lakes — create a branch, run experiments, merge if successful. FAANG practice: Google uses Goodle's own data versioning for TF training data. Uber's ML platform stores all training data snapshots in Hive/Spark DataFrames with timestamp-based versioning.
sections.codeBlock:
# AI/ML database patterns

# pgvector — create and query embeddings
CREATE TABLE items (id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY, content TEXT, embedding vector(1536));
CREATE INDEX ON items USING hnsw(embedding vector_cosine_ops);

# Query similar items
SELECT id, content, embedding <=> '[0.01, 0.02, ...]' AS distance
FROM items ORDER BY distance LIMIT 10;

# Feature store — Feast online serving
# from feast import FeatureStore
# store = FeatureStore(repo_path="./feature_repo")
# features = store.get_online_features(
#     features=["user:age", "user:signup_week"],
#     entity_rows=[{"user_id": 1234}]
# ).to_dict()

# DVC — data versioning
dvc add data/training_set.parquet
git add data/training_set.parquet.dvc
git commit -m "add training data v4"

sections.title: Cost Modeling & FinOps for Databases
sections.description:
COST MODELING · FINOPS · CAPACITY PLANNING

Staff+ expectation: At FAANG scale, database costs run millions per year. Understand storage/compute/IO pricing models, reserved vs on-demand tradeoffs, data transfer costs, right-sizing strategies, and how to attribute costs per team/database. Staff+ engineers make build-vs-buy decisions that save millions.

Database Cost Components:

  • Compute: Instance size (vCPU + RAM). AWS: db.r6g.16xlarge (64 vCPU, 512GB RAM) ~$5.50/hr on-demand, ~$3.30/hr reserved (3yr). At 10 instances: $2M+/year on-demand vs $1.2M reserved. Staff+ insight: Reserved instances save 40-60%. For variable workloads, use RDS Savings Plans (similar to reserved, but more flexible).
  • Storage: Provisioned GB/month. EBS gp3: $0.08/GB-month. Aurora: $0.10/GB-month (includes 6-way replication). A 10TB database on Aurora costs ~$12K/month just for storage. Optimization: Use S3 tiering for cold data (PostgreSQL with s3_fdw, Aurora Backtrack, archive to S3 Glacier at $0.004/GB-month).
  • I/O: Aurora I/O cost: $0.20 per million I/O requests. A write-heavy workload at 10K writes/sec costs ~$500/month in I/O charges alone. Optimization: Use Aurora I/O-Optimized configuration if I/O is predictable (no per-I/O charges, fixed 30% premium on instance).
  • Data transfer: Cross-AZ: $0.01/GB each way. A replication stream of 100GB/hour between AZs costs ~$700/month. Optimization: Colocate read replicas in the same AZ to avoid transfer costs. Use cross-region replication judiciously — it costs $0.02/GB.

Cost Attribution & Showback: (1) Tag every database with team, environment, service. (2) Use AWS Cost Explorer / GCP Billing to attribute costs per tag. (3) Charge teams proportionally: share cost per DB = (instance compute + storage + I/O) / number of tenants. Staff+ insight: The largest hidden cost is underutilization. Run CloudWatch CPUUtilization for all DB instances — if CPU <20% for >90% of the time, the instance is oversized. Right-sizing can cut 40-60% of compute costs.
sections.codeBlock:
# Database cost optimization at FAANG scale

# Check CPU utilization for right-sizing (CloudWatch)
# SELECT AVG(CPUUtilization) FROM AWS/RDS WHERE DBInstanceIdentifier='my-db'
# If <20% for 90th percentile: downsize

# Aurora I/O cost calculation
# Writes per second: 10,000
# Monthly I/O: 10,000 * 86,400 * 30 = 25.9B I/Os
# Cost: 25.9B * $0.20 / 1M = $5,184/month

# Reserved instance calculation
# On-demand: db.r6g.16xlarge @ $5.50/hr = $4,818/month
# 3yr reserved: $3.30/hr = $2,890/month
# Savings: $1,928/month per instance

# S3 archiving for cold data
# PostgreSQL with s3_fdw
CREATE FOREIGN TABLE archived_orders (...) SERVER s3_server
OPTIONS (dir 's3://my-bucket/db_archive/orders/');

sections.title: Staff+ Interview Strategy & System Design with DB Depth
sections.description:
INTERVIEW PATTERNS · STRATEGY · DEPTH

Staff+ expectation: Database questions in Staff+ interviews embed DB depth into system design. The interviewer wants to see you reason about storage engine selection, replication consistency, sharding strategy, and operational concerns — not just draw boxes.

Common Staff+ Design Questions with Database Depth:

  • "Design a globally distributed user database for a social network" — DB angles: (1) Sharding key = user ID (consistency of access), (2) Replication — single-leader per region, async cross-region (AP-style for global, CP-style within region), (3) Consistency — monotonic reads via read-your-writes from primary region; eventual for non-critical, (4) Storage engine — B-tree for user profiles (frequent updates), LSM for activity feeds (write-heavy, append-mostly), (5) Migration strategy — online resharding with Vitess.
  • "Design a time-series metrics platform" — DB angles: (1) Storage — columnar (TimescaleDB hypertables, ClickHouse) for efficient range scans, (2) Retention — downsampling (rollups at 1h/1d granularity), (3) Write throughput — batch writes (1K-10K points per insert), async writes with in-memory buffers, (4) Partitioning — time-based with range partitioning, (5) Compression — out-of-the-box columnar compression (10-50x).
  • "Design a multi-region ordering system" — DB angles: (1) Distributed transactions — Saga pattern (no 2PC), (2) Partitioning — by region (data lives close to users), (3) Replication — async cross-region with conflict resolution (last-write-wins or CRDT), (4) Idempotency — idempotency keys for order creation (prevent double-charge), (5) Eventual consistency — accept that inventory count may be stale between regions.

Build vs Buy Decision Framework: (1) Build a custom storage engine? Only if you need multi-dimensional ordering or custom consistency models that no off-the-shelf DB provides. Google built Spanner (no alternative existed at scale). Amazon built DynamoDB (needed predictable single-digit-ms at any scale). For most use cases, PostgreSQL (with extensions) or DynamoDB covers 90% of needs. (2) When to use a managed service? Always for low-touch workloads (RDS, Aurora, DynamoDB). Self-managed when you need: custom kernel tuning, unusual hardware (Optane, FPGA), or regulatory compliance that requires on-prem. (3) Operational maturity: Each self-managed database requires a team of experts (DBA + SRE). Budget ~2 FTE per 100 database instances.

FAANG Company-Specific Focus: Google asks about Spanner and Bigtable internals. Amazon asks about DynamoDB partitioning and DAX caching. Meta asks about MySQL at scale (gh-ost, MyRocks, TAO). Netflix asks about cross-region replication and chaos engineering for databases.
sections.codeBlock:
# Staff+ interview preparation checklist

# Must-know papers:
# - Spanner: TrueTime and External Consistency (OSDI 2012)
# - Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store (SOSP 2007)
# - Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System for Structured Data (OSDI 2006)
# - Aurora: Amazon Aurora (SIGMOD 2017)
# - The Case for Learned Index Structures (Kraska, 2018)

# Key numbers to memorize:
# - B-tree fanout with 8KB pages: ~341 keys per node
# - B-tree search depth: ~4 for 1B rows
# - LSM write amplification: 10-30x (leveled)
# - Aurora write quorum: 4/6 across 3 AZs
# - Spanner TrueTime interval: 1-7ms

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
Staff+ database engineering requires reading deeply from both academic papers and production engineering blogs. Below are the canonical references organized by topic.

TopicResourceWhy for Staff+
FoundationalDesigning Data-Intensive Applications (Kleppmann, 2017)Chapters 1-6: storage engines, replication, partitioning, transactions — the definitive database reference. Every Staff+ engineer should know chapters 5-9 cover to cover.
TransactionsARIES: A Transaction Recovery Method (Moham et al, 1992) & Critique of ANSI SQL Isolation Levels (Berenson et al, 1995)The two papers that define how databases recover from crashes and what isolation actually means. Essential for any Staff+ engineer designing DB internals.
Distributed SystemsSpanner: Google's Globally-Distributed Database (OSDI 2012) & In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft) (USENIX ATC 2014)Spanner defines the state of the art in global SQL. Raft is the consensus algorithm used in etcd, TiDB, CockroachDB, and many FAANG internal systems.
StorageThe Log-Structured Merge-Tree (LSM-Tree) (O'Neil et al, 1996) & B-trees: The Key to Everything (Graefe, 2011)The original LSM paper and the definitive B-tree reference. Graefe's survey covers every B-tree variant used in production databases.
Cloud DBsAmazon Aurora: Design Considerations for High Throughput Cloud-Native RDBMS (SIGMOD 2017) & Amazon DynamoDB: A Scalable, Predictably Performant, and Fully Managed NoSQL DB (SIGMOD 2022)Aurora's disaggregated storage architecture set the standard for cloud databases. DynamoDB's design choices (partitioning, consistency, DAX) define the NoSQL cloud market.
Query OptimizationAccess Path Selection in a Relational Database Management System (Selinger et al, 1979) & How Good Are Query Optimizers, Really? (Leis et al, 2015)The Selinger paper is the foundation of all modern query optimizers. Leis proves that even the best optimizers make mistakes — essential humility for Staff+ engineers tuning queries.
ML for DBThe Case for Learned Index Structures (Kraska et al, 2018) & Neo: A Learned Query Optimizer (Marcus et al, 2019)Kraska's paper shattered the assumption that indexes must be trees. Neo showed that deep learning can replace traditional query optimization. Both are Staff+ discussion topics.
